intro:
Gideon (Hebrew Gidon, "hewer" or "one who cuts down"), also called Jerubbaal, was one of the great judges of Israel, whose story is told at length in Judges 6–8. The son of Joash of the clan of Abiezer in the tribe of Manasseh, he was raised up to deliver Israel after seven years of crushing oppression by the Midianites, who swept in each harvest like locusts and stripped the land bare (Judges 6:1–6). His career is a study in how God works mighty deliverance through a fearful and unlikely instrument.

The angel of the LORD found Gideon threshing wheat in a winepress to hide it from the raiders, and greeted him, "The LORD is with you, O mighty man of valour" (Judges 6:12). Gideon protested that his clan was the weakest and he the least in his father's house, but God promised, "I will be with you, and you shall strike the Midianites as one man" (Judges 6:15–16). After a sign of fire consuming his offering, he tore down his father's altar of Baal and the Asherah beside it by night — for which the townsmen nicknamed him Jerubbaal, "let Baal contend with him" (Judges 6:25–32). Still seeking assurance, he twice tested God with the fleece, asking for dew first on the wool and then on the ground (Judges 6:36–40).

When Gideon mustered thirty-two thousand men, the LORD pared the army down to a mere three hundred, "lest Israel boast... saying, 'My own hand has saved me'" (Judges 7:2–7). With trumpets, torches hidden in clay jars, and the shout "A sword for the LORD and for Gideon!", his tiny band surrounded the Midianite camp by night; the enemy host panicked, turned their swords on one another, and fled (Judges 7:19–22). Gideon pursued them across the Jordan, capturing the princes Oreb and Zeeb and the kings Zebah and Zalmunna (Judges 7:25; Judges 8:12).

In victory Gideon rose to his noblest moment. When Israel begged him to be their king and found a dynasty, he refused: "I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you; the LORD will rule over you" (Judges 8:23) — a clear confession of God's kingship. Yet his later life was clouded: from the gold of the spoil he made an ephod that "became a snare to Gideon and to his family," drawing Israel into idolatry (Judges 8:27). The land had rest forty years while he lived, but after his death the nation turned again to Baal, and his son Abimelech seized power by murdering his brothers, plunging Israel into bloodshed (Judges 9:5). Despite these shadows, Gideon is honoured in the New Testament among the heroes of faith who "through faith conquered kingdoms" and "out of weakness were made strong" (Hebrews 11:32–34). His life stands as a lasting testimony that the battle belongs to the LORD, who chooses the weak things to shame the strong, and a sober warning that even great faith must guard against compromise.
hitchcock_meaning: he that bruises or breaks; a destroyer
